title: Consciousness Explained
author: Daniel C. Dennett
themes: Consciousness is not a mysterious extra — it is a set of computational processes in the brain
The 'Cartesian Theater' — the idea that there is a central place where consciousness happens — is an illusion
Multiple drafts model: consciousness is the activity of parallel, distributed processes in the brain, not a unified stream
Qualia — the subjective feel of experience — are not as real or mysterious as philosophers claim
The self is a 'center of narrative gravity,' not a metaphysical entity
Heterophenomenology: taking the subject's reports seriously without assuming their accuracy
excerpt: Consciousness seems to be the last bastion of occult properties, of magical powers, of ghostly presences, the only place where the mind-body problem remains truly formidable. But it is a natural phenomenon — a product of the brain's computational activities. There is no separate 'consciousness' that must be added to the brain's functions. Consciousness is the brain's functions, properly understood.
